steps.title: State the hypothesis precisely
steps.content: The researcher predicted that Arctic ground squirrels (hibernate alone) would have longer torpor bouts and shorter arousal episodes than Alaska marmots (hibernate in groups). Restated from the marmots’ perspective: marmots should have shorter torpor bouts and longer arousal episodes than ground squirrels. The correct answer must match both parts of this prediction with actual table data.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — The Alaska marmots' arousal episodes lasted for days, while the Arctic ground squirrels' arousal episodes lasted less than a day.: Marmot arousal episodes averaged 21.2 hours (under a day), not “days”—this misreads the table and ignores torpor bout comparison. ✗
  • Choice B — The Alaska marmots and the Arctic ground squirrels both maintained torpor for several consecutive days per bout, on average.: This notes only that both had multi-day torpor bouts; it makes no cross-species comparison and omits arousal episodes entirely. ✗
  • Choice C — The Alaska marmots had shorter torpor bouts and longer arousal episodes than the Arctic ground squirrels did.: Marmots averaged 13.81 days per torpor bout vs. 16.77 days for ground squirrels (shorter); marmots averaged 21.2 hours per arousal episode vs. 14.2 hours (longer). Both parts of the hypothesis are confirmed. ✓
  • Choice D — The Alaska marmots had more torpor bouts than arousal episodes, but their arousal episodes were much shorter than their torpor bouts.: This compares marmot behaviors only against each other; it never compares marmots to ground squirrels, so it cannot support the hypothesis. ✗
That matches Choice C — The Alaska marmots had shorter torpor bouts and longer arousal episodes than the Arctic ground squirrels did..
